Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B ,
Happy Monday! I hope you all had a great holiday, and that it=E2=80=99s not=
 too rough getting back into the flow of things this morning.=20
Every year, on New Year=E2=80=99s Day, I take some time to reflect back on =
the previous year and set some goals for the year ahead. This time last yea=
r, I was in the middle of starting an in-person community business and writ=
ing this newsletter=E2=80=99s predecessor as a fun side thing. I set a goal=
 to hit 1,000 subscribers by the end of 2020.=20
Here we are on the first Monday of 2021, and there are more than 29,000 of =
us here, and all of my professional focus and goals are centered on this th=
ing.=20
One of the goals I wrote down is to keep improving the content, pushing mys=
elf, and trying new things. In that spirit, why not kick off the year with =
something I didn=E2=80=99t do at all last year: a bear case.=20
Let=E2=80=99s get to it.=20
Bill-A-Bear
It was the fifth email in a long back-and-forth with a sponsor that changed=
 me, made me bearish on a company not named =E2=80=9CQuibi=E2=80=9D for onc=
e.=C2=A0
Here=E2=80=99s how it went down: a company sponsored Not Boring. They wante=
d to pay me, asked me to invoice them on bill.com. Wham, bam, thank you sir=
=2E But they kept trying to pay me and t=
hey kept getting notifications that i=
t didn=E2=80=99t work. I kept trying to fix it on my end, even upgraded to =
a paid plan, kept telling them that it was fixed.=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CShould work now!=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CNope. Maybe you should contact customer support.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
=E2=80=9COK got it, all squared away. Try again!=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CIt didn=E2=80=99t work.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
Finally, on the fifth try, we got it, but not before a strange and unusual =
feeling set in: I was officially bearish on bill.com.

down of SoftBank, I came away kind of loving Masa [https://email.mg2.substa=

What is bill.com?=C2=A0
Bill.com is founder Ren=C3=A9 Lacerte=E2=80=99s second bite at the apple.=

Lacerte=E2=80=99s next thing, which he founded in 2006, was originally call=

Bill.com=E2=80=99s mission was and is to =E2=80=9Cmake it simple to connect=
 and do business.=E2=80=9D It attempts to do that through cloud-based Accou=
nts Receivable (AR) and Accounts Payable (AP) software for SMBs.=C2=A0
If it sounds unsexy, that=E2=80=99s because it is, but it=E2=80=99s also im=
portant. In any business, particularly SMBs, cash is king. A company can im=
prove its cash position without doing a dollar more in sales or cutting any=
 costs by stretching out payables and pulling in receivables. There=E2=80=
=99s even a formula for it: the Cash Conversion Cycle formula.=C2=A0
Bill=E2=80=99s promise is that by giving customers better tools and data, c=
ompanies will receive money more quickly and pay out more slowly, shortenin=
g the CCC and putting more money in the bank at any given time.=C2=A0
The prize for helping companies simplify AR and AP and improve the cash con=
version cycle is large: there are 6 million small or medium businesses (SMB=
s), those with revenues under $100 million, in the United States, and 75-90=
% of those businesses still pay for things with checks. Over the next decad=
e, most of that volume will move online.=C2=A0
The question is: how do you capture it?=C2=A0
In tech, there=E2=80=99s a never-ending battle between product (the actual =
software) and distribution (how a company sells to customers). Bill.com is =
a distribution play.=C2=A0
Lacerte ran essentially the same playbook at PayCycle and bill.com:
Replace a paper-based finance workflow with software.
Distribute through partnerships with accounting firms and others who own th=
e relationships with SMB clients.=C2=A0
Try to create network effects and switching costs and hold on for dear life.
Because PayCycle and bill.com competed with physically writing out paycheck=
s and invoices, they had a low product quality bar to jump over. So they bu=
ilt something functional and tried to get it in the hands of as many people=
 as possible as quickly as possible in a race to create network effects.=C2=

Business Model
Bill.com=E2=80=99s business model is straightforward. It makes money in thr=
ee ways:=C2=A0
Subscription (53% of revenue). Monthly plans start as low as $39/mo/user.=
=C2=A0=C2=A0
Transactions (33% of revenue). Bill collects fees for every transaction com=
pleted through the platform.=C2=A0
Float (13% of revenue). Bill earns interest while payments are clearing. In=
 my experience, that takes about a week each time.=C2=A0
The business model is strong on paper: it combines the predictability of mo=
nthly recurring revenue with the upside of transaction fees. It grows as it=
 adds new customers, as its customers add more users, and as they make or a=
ccept more payments through the platform.=C2=A0
In the 12 months ended September 2020, it brought in $169 million in revenu=
e, 86% of which came from subscription and transaction fees (float tanked a=
s interest rates did). 86% of that core revenue came from existing customer=
s, thanks to a strong 121% dollar-based net retention rate, a good sign tha=
t the company is able to lock in customers and grow with them. The network =
effects and switching costs seem to be working.=C2=A0
The numbers show a core business that=E2=80=99s solid if not spectacular. A=
nalysts cite potential upside from new products like virtual card issuance,=
 cross-border transactions, and instant transfer. The 121% dollar-based net=
 retention rate is a good signal that the distribution =E2=86=92 network ef=
fect lock-in strategy is working.
The market seems to like what it sees. Bill.com went public on December 12,=
 2019 at a $1.6 billion market cap on Q1 2020 revenues of $35.2 million and=

s of $46.2 million (31% higher) and net losses of $12.9 million (56% higher=
), BILL is trading at an $11.1 billion market cap (7x higher)!=C2=A0
Something=E2=80=99s off. I don=E2=80=99t think anyone on Wall Street has ev=
er actually had to use bill.com=E2=80=A6=C2=A0

0%. Square=E2=80=99s revenue is weird, because when people buy Bitcoin on C=
ash App, it books the full amount as revenue, so to be charitable to bill.c=
om, let=E2=80=99s look at gross profits.=C2=A0
Square grew gross profits 59% YoY to $794 million in Q3 2020, accelerating =

Square was profitable last quarter, with $36.5 million in net income. Bill.=
com was twice as unprofitable as it was the same quarter the previous year,=

Bill.com is growing more slowly than Square (and that growth is deceleratin=
g) off a much smaller base. Its product is worse. It doesn=E2=80=99t have t=
he talent density or design focus that Square does. It serves a much smalle=
r customer base, putting it at a distribution disadvantage. It doesn=E2=80=

ized Revenue is 54.6x, Square=E2=80=99s EV / Annualized Gross Profit is 30.=
9x. That=E2=80=99s crazy.=C2=A0
Here=E2=80=99s what I think is happening, and believe me, I know this sound=

=80=99t feel like a product built in or for this decade, and it=E2=80=99s g=

The Product
Bill.com is the worst software I use to run Not Boring. It takes something =
that should be joyful -- getting paid! -- and makes it dreadful. That prese=
nts an opening for product-first competitors to cut off bill.com=E2=80=99s =
growth and pick off customers.=C2=A0
The idea for bill.com=E2=80=99s product sounds simple -- send and receive i=
nvoices and payments online -- and the product looks clean enough on the su=
rface, but in my experience, there are a host of little things that add up =
to a poor product experience.=C2=A0
Poor Communication. When a sponsor pays me, there=E2=80=99s no communicatio=
n or notification. The money just kind of floats in the ether. To find out =
where it is, I need to contact them through the =E2=80=9CMessage Center.=E2=
=80=9D
Confusing UI. Often, a sponsor will pay me outside of bill.com, and the inv=
oice sits there, marked as unpaid and overdue. It took me half an hour of c=
licking around to figure out how to mark the invoice =E2=80=9CPaid.=E2=80=
=9D
Issues with Multiple Bank Accounts. One of my bank accounts was approved, a=
nother wasn=E2=80=99t, or something, and when I tried to switch, without wa=
rning, I was no longer able to receive payments.=C2=A0
Poor Onboarding and Late Verification. Instead of verifying my identity upf=
ront, I got a message in my Message Center asking me to upload my driver=E2=
=80=99s license and incorporation docs after I=E2=80=99d already completed =
a few transactions and was in the middle of another one, delaying payment. =
This could have been solved by a walkthrough at signup, a very normal featu=
re in modern software.=C2=A0
Hard to Connect Directly with Vendor. Unless you have the right email addre=
ss upfront when you create a vendor account, good luck figuring out how to =
connect to the right vendor.
There are more, but you get the point. To be clear, none of these issues is=
 insurmountable, and if I worked in AP I=E2=80=99m sure I=E2=80=99d spend t=
he time to get really good at bill.com, but in 2021, the counterintuitivene=
ss of the product is striking.=C2=A0
Modern software, even B2B SaaS, is supposed to be intuitive. Magical little=
 details that work better than expected are the norm. That=E2=80=99s the pr=
omise of the consumerization of enterprise SaaS: finally, work products tha=
t we love as much as the products we choose to use as consumers.=C2=A0

The Love-Hate Relationship with Work Software
There=E2=80=99s a risk to the bill.com bear case: the market loves the comp=
anies behind the B2B products people hate using.
Last week, I asked Twitter for the worst software they have to use for work=
:=C2=A0
I got a lot of answers. People feel strongly about the shit they=E2=80=99re=
 forced to use at work every day. Out of hundreds of answers, a few leaders=
 emerged. Salesforce is the most-hated single product, but nothing holds a =
candle to Microsoft=E2=80=99s combined suite - Dynamics, 365, PowerPoint, O=
utlook, Windows 95, Teams, and myriad other gems got nearly 5x the votes of=
 the next company. Powered by Jira and Confluence, that silver medal goes t=
o Atlassian.
But here=E2=80=99s the thing. Over the past five years, the companies that =
make the most hated work software have performed really well=E2=80=A6=C2=A0
The best performer and second-most-hated, Atlassian (TEAM) is up a whopping=
 753%. On average, the group is up 231.6%, compared with 82.6% for the S&P =
500 and 157.4% for the Nasdaq. The only negative performer on the list, IBM=
, is only there because it bought Lotus Notes, and people still hate Lotus =
Notes.=C2=A0
If you bought an equally-weighted basket of the companies that make the sof=
tware that business users hate the most five years ago (and added when newl=
y public companies IPO=E2=80=99d), you would have outperformed the S&P by 4=
x and the Nasdaq by half! That=E2=80=99s a good hedge fund. How is that pos=
sible?=C2=A0
There are a few reasons.=C2=A0
First things first, to beat the naysayers to it, this data is incredibly me=
ssy and imprecise. My twitter universe is not a representative sample. Some=
 of these companies are massive and also make products that people love. Mi=
crosoft users hate Windows, Teams, and Dynamics, but they love Excel. Slack=

n era in which purchasing decisions were made centrally and the end-users, =

S] it, SAP=E2=80=99s switching costs mean, =E2=80=9CSwitching to another so=
lution can be a months-long effort costing several millions of dollars, and=
 much more in missed profits if done wrong.=E2=80=9D
This is what SAP=E2=80=99s software looks like, for the uninitiated.
It takes some mighty powerful switching costs to keep people using somethin=
g like that. Those companies have decades of moat-building to protect them =
against plucky startups.=C2=A0
Today, though, the product versus distribution debate presents a false dich=
otomy. A product has to be thoughtfully-designed, and increasingly, distrib=
ution is built into the product itself. I also asked Twitter for their favo=
rite work software, and the winners are mainly from this new breed (Excel i=
s a rare beast):=C2=A0
For the most part, they=E2=80=99re product-first companies with distributio=
n baked into the product, and later turbocharged by strong sales teams.=C2=
=A0
Notion [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOhSAM_ZrL0vCQERYsZjO_QRCqkq=
tgAMf499Ori0maNm3Tc3qOdw3mXC6z59rIJ9l27YBt8A4nR4ViYzDsiyshe02C6QNTUpFY7VQAN=

7fUlvl_8B-M8zy7lD3pXM_YkGk45o4z2rBdMyo51Sg1rmHcqgn_1dJt5V4-xNuffnc8bKQYBxlx=
imnH9XtwG673A1y3W7UixXRaSG1cIj6r2OHPrtDMkKOhYsK6hJVQPAx2wKvaoQNmCaqE4YwSZQ8=
ar9E_6B7uKes4] is designed for both private and public use. Every time some=
one exposes a portion of their Notion as a public-facing website, for examp=
le, the product spreads.=C2=A0
Slack [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxVkM1qxSAQhZ_mukvwtzELF930NYI_00S=
u0aCmkLfv9AZaCuowHsfD-bztsJZ6maO0Tn6OpV8HYBu8xZuzQV1iMOyNa6HkTIKRgWmlSWzLZw=

fejPcT7g3_gyqW7UmNex3a61q1_jr7sKBy4W8J-6BsM7kypDbbCgDnwNYmGU84oo5JJwZQa2aj1=
lMJ6UBH8Q9J95f--JNX8eqH83OwO6SVgwgXrfubYrwWydQnCHb7fAF84lhUyVAQbFtuRHJ2niU5=
YNbvDIh1BkQRnjKBzKDiV_0y_AdOQiJY] built Slack Connect to help the product s=
pread from company to company.=C2=A0
Figma [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOwyAM_JpyjMwjDRw47KW_gQg4KWo=
CESFb5e_XbQ4rWbbG1mg8E3zDudTTbmVv7NNcOzckGIOnzbFjdSlafhda9sqwaFXkutcs7W6qiK=
tPi231QLYd45KCb6nkLwNA9OxplUERxT3Ie0CjpmnSpg_RSBhRgYbxEvVHTJgDWvzFepaMbLHP1=
rb9Jn9u4kH1fr-7Kc2r70JZCbNkBQgOHBRXkvd9xzuthyXOG8gYbgrWWXT7Me7Nh9eHxKrNpY2l=
pjzT-fX0Ky7fA73uaK5HTu10mP24YLxctSuZr083Y8ZKiUXnG0UCZhhgoKn55YJsSzBSC84ZKcd=
CrPwv-gfZBnrR]=E2=80=99s whole product is designed with growth loops in min=
d. (If you haven=E2=80=99t, you should read Kevin Kwok=E2=80=99s Why Figma =
Wins [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOhCAQ_JrhtoaXAx447GV_gyC0SlQw=
gDvx77dnPGxCqtLddFeqvGsw53KZI9dG3mDbdQCWwTvsnBWKjcGwJ9eilwMJRgame01itVMB2F3=
cTCsnkOMct-hdizl9NijlPVkMKKFCrwaulWRBMBicknqU3Llp6hm_Rd0ZIiQPBn6hXDkB2czS2l=
Ef4vvBf_Ctr7z6xcXU-bxjzSmnSPSJwAaE13J9TXHe3dcrpooNEg1-YpRRyaRgfd-xTmu1hfmgI=
viHpPvMu3qOtTm_vs-SYlJuYy4xzTheF7fD9hmgOYu8nym2y0Jy4wbh9t3u7D5J2BkSFMw0WNcw=
NDooRRWyZrdPDEbQQWjOGEHlkHEr_Yv-AeeAg-Q] now.)
While most of the companies on the list are still private and don=E2=80=99t=
 report numbers, customer love seems to correspond to venture funding, whic=
h should be at least a rough proxy for company performance:=C2=A0
In April, both Notion and Figma hit $2 billion valuations.=C2=A0

yaAmEBFSKbevZ7KoZNky1tPjezF0XGq73F6Pzt7N92tHWlMM9HIe2HxOjo_CSK0sS04lbrRh-fD=

8m_6Bw5feUw] raised $185 million at a $2.5 billion valuation in September.
ClickUp [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOwyAM_JpyjHiEQg4c9rK_gQy4K=
WoCESFb5e_X2xxWsmx5rNF4JkLHubbTbXXv7K_5fm5Ia4pAyLFj8zk5cZdW6XFiyY1JWG1Z3v2j=

n79t-U183-U31fr-HSAKvYxtiXQlh2UkuBRd8FKMSWg9isNYsad64SvE28nWWw36EvUN8_ZFYc6=
X2UFsuM51fT1hx-RzoeU9zPUrup8cCYcF0-epXNh-nfsaCjTJLHjqFwidjuKFpxeWDjCs-KSuFY=
KScKrHKv-gvs5R7bw] raised $100 million at a $1 billion valuation in Decembe=
r.=C2=A0
Roam [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOwyAM_JpyjHiEQg4c9tLfQA44CWoC=
ESEr5e_XbQ4rWbY81mg8E6DhXOrl9nI09mm-XTvSGgMQch5YfYpOPKVVuh9YdH0UVluWDj9VxA3=
S6lo9ke3nuKYALZX8ZXAuNVucmYIRSk0wKRU4SAP2qSfTcw2DVdbconDGhDmgw1-sV8nIVre0tj=
_Uz0O-qGqBreKBUMPShbIRxJKTXAoueC96JbTuRGetWeO8cxXDo-fbLLvjHI8G4f0hsepyaWOpK=
c90fi-w4fo90POe5nbm1C6PGcYV4-2r3dl8nfoZM1bKLHpoFAofjOGGphW3DzKu-KCsFIKRcizE=
yv-if3Tbey4] raised a seed round at a $200 million valuation in September.=
=C2=A0=C2=A0
On the public side, Slack was the fastest SaaS company ever to hit $100 mil=
lion in revenue, and recently sold to Salesforce (SAD!) for $27.7 billion.=
=C2=A0
We all know what happened to Zoom=E2=80=99s stock in 2020 (just in case: it=
 rose 395%).=C2=A0
Product-first with built-in distribution is the new model. Customers love i=
t. Investors love it.=C2=A0
A distribution-first company like bill.com would never even get off the gro=
und in 2021. We=E2=80=99re past that. That brings us to the concluding ques=
tion: is it locked in enough at this point that it=E2=80=99s going to stick=
 around forever like Microsoft, Salesforce, and Oracle, or do competitors s=
till have a chance to take it down?=C2=A0
Killing a Baby Dictator
There=E2=80=99s a question that ethicists like to pose over a couple of dri=
nks: if you could go back in time and kill a dictator / terrorist / murdere=
r when they were still a baby, would you do it?=C2=A0
We can=E2=80=99t go back and kill SAP, Salesforce, or Microsoft. The switch=
ing costs are too high, the moats are too deep, and companies are too locke=
d in. Those companies=E2=80=99 customers will have to die out for their pro=
ducts to die out. But it=E2=80=99s not too late to stop bill.com.=C2=A0
I think what ultimately offends me so much about Bill=E2=80=99s product exp=
erience is that it=E2=80=99s the first time I=E2=80=99ve encountered a new =
work product that wasn=E2=80=99t well-designed in a long time.=C2=A0
I expect Salesforce to suck. It=E2=80=99s been around for a while, since be=
fore the software we use at work was supposed to be good, and it=E2=80=99s =
too late to do anything but build better interfaces on top. Salesforce got =
big enough, fast enough that we=E2=80=99re stuck with it.=C2=A0
But bill.com has a 2006-vintage product with slow enough growth that most p=
otential customers are just finding out about it in 2020. It only has 103k =
customers in a market that it says is 6 million potential customers strong.=
 For anyone, like me and the 5.9 million other target customers just discov=
ering bill.com from today onward, the product experience is jarring. It=E2=
=80=99s like walking into a Mercedes dealership today and having the salesp=
erson pass off an obviously used 2006 E-Class as the newest model.=C2=A0
Bill.com has left a wide opening for new entrants to come in and build bett=
er AR/AP software that makes getting paid fun and helps companies better ma=
nage their cash conversion cycles.=C2=A0
To knock off bill.com, Square, Stripe, Settle, or a new, product-first comp=
etitor will need to:=C2=A0
Build a seamless product to feature parity with bill.com on the things that=
 matter to customers.=C2=A0
Build distribution into the product workflows.=C2=A0
Build up enough customer demand to attract enough vendors and partners onto=
 the platform that Bill=E2=80=99s switching costs and network effects are r=
endered obsolete.=C2=A0=C2=A0
With those companies=E2=80=99 product cadence and 5.9 million customers rip=
e for the picking, many of whom have moved online during the pandemic, I li=
ke their odds. When they do, they=E2=80=99ll limit Bill=E2=80=99s upside an=
d make it impossible for the company to grow into its impossibly high valua=
tion.=C2=A0
Even after a wild run in 2020, I=E2=80=99m still incredibly bullish on tech=
 companies. Product-first companies with built-in distribution will create =
the next generation of $100 billion+ market cap giants. Bet on those compan=
ies, even if their market caps are much higher than bill.com=E2=80=99s.
As for bill.com? I=E2=80=99m not shorting, because shorting anything in a m=
arket like this is asking to burn money, and there=E2=80=99s always the ris=
k that one of the old guard sees a younger version of itself in bill.com=E2=
=80=99s product and buys it.=C2=A0
Instead, I=E2=80=99m considering selling long-dated OTM calls. Heads, bill.=
com tanks or stays flat, I win. Tails, bill.com rockets another 50%, and I =
lose some money, but I can only imagine how well the other companies in my =
portfolio would be doing. It=E2=80=99s a small hedge on my very overweight =
tech portfolio.=C2=A0
Plus, if the trade works out, it will be the easiest time I=E2=80=99ve ever=
 had getting paid via bill.com=E2=80=A6=C2=A0

nt-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Every year, on New Ye=

-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">One of the goals I wrot=

azonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F19e430fe-7cc1-46f2-9072-df39d98c756e_971x35=

isplay: block; height: auto; margin: 0 auto; margin-bottom: 0; max-width: 5=
50px; vertical-align: middle; width: 100%;"></a></td><td style=3D"text-alig=
n: center;"></td></tr></tbody></table></figure></div><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Finally, the =
growling got loud enough that I decided to look at the numbers. Surely, if =
I felt this way, others did too. That must be reflected in the stock price,=
 right? Wrong. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-h=
eight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill’s stock price nearly <strong>qu=
adrupled </strong>last year, and the numbers don’t support the move. =
Bill is a bottom-quartile BVP Emerging Cloud Index company trading like a t=
op quartile one. Its revenue growth is slowing, its revenue multiple: dizzy=
ing, its free cash flow: negative. It faces competition from powerful incum=
bents, fintech darlings like Stripe and Square, and new startups like <a hr=
ef=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMtuxCAM_JrlGPFIAjlw6GV_AxFws2gJ=
ROBUyt_X3RwqWX5qNJ4JHmGr7bJH7cj-ksPrABpj8LQ5OzSXohWzNGoaFxbtGIWZDEvdfTeA3ad=
ssZ3AjnPNKXhMtXwQnMuJvazU3AQtFZ8ns-o5zHIFNavogxIcFn2T-jMmKAEs_EC7agGW7Qvx6A=
_19ZBPig6IGYZQqWfJSi4FF3wUoxLTNIjBGJ3jdnAVw2Pk-yaHfq4dfXgTZmfNloprbalsdH6__=
A75c6C3HdX9LAkvB8WvGeKtCG9XPhrdBgUauRWdR7KDL1pzTdWIWwFJVnxRRgrBiDlWQpV_0l8L=
TXiO" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">Settle</a>. And=
 did I mention its product? It feels like something a Salesforce Product Ma=
nager would dream up if Salesforce enacted Google-like 20% projects. <=
/p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: =
0 0 1em 0;">(<em>Ed. note: I learned, after writing this sentence, that ind=
eed, bill.com’s first two engineers came from Salesforce and the comp=
any bought the domain from Marc Benioff. If it walks like a Salesforce and =
quacks like a Salesforce, or something.)</em></p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a=
; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Now look, I don&#=
8217;t want to pick on bill.com. The company’s 618 employees are tryi=
ng their best, and they’ve built a company worth over $11 billion. I,=
 as a reminder, write a free newsletter. It brings me no joy to pop the Bil=
l bubble. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height=
: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But having a bad experience with bill.com was l=
ucky. It forced me to sharpen my bear claws heading into a year during whic=
h sharp bear claws will come in handy.  </p><p style=3D"color: #1=
a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">While 2020 w=
as a year for <em>unbridled</em> tech optimism, I think 2021 is going to be=
 a year for <em>discerning</em> tech optimism. There’s going to be a =
flight to quality within tech. Market leaders with strong growth, big margi=
ns, profitability, and best-in-class products will continue to earn strong =
multiples, but laggards with outdated products and relatively unattractive =
numbers will underperform, regardless of sub-sector or market cap. </p=
><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 =
0 1em 0;">This whole bear thing is new to me, so we’ll take it one st=
ep at a time:</p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0=
 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>What is bill.com? </st=
rong>A just-the-facts look at Bill’s product, business model, and dis=
tribution.</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-=
format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: =
26px; margin: 0;"><strong>The Bill Bear Case. </strong>Bill.com touts the h=
ighest revenue multiple in the entire BVP Cloud Index, and it has neither t=
he numbers nor the product to back it up. </p></li></ul><ul style=3D"m=
argin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-form=
at: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0;"><strong>The Love-Hate Relationship with Work Software.</stron=
g> People hate old, locked-in enterprise software, and love the new breed o=
f product-first, consumerized B2B SaaS. Bill.com’s product fits with =
the old guard, but it hasn’t had as much time as them to dig moats.</=
p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bull=
et;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin=
: 0;"><strong>Killing a Baby Dictator. </strong>Bill.com is still small eno=
ugh for competitors to defeat before it’s too late. </p></li></u=
l><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0=
 0 1em 0;">As always, this is not investment advice. I’m definitely n=
ot suggesting that you short a tech momentum stock in this market. The Keyn=
es quote that, “The markets can remain irrational longer than you can=
 remain solvent” rings truer every day. </p><h3 style=3D"color: =
#1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFo=
nt, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, 'Apple Color Emoji', =
'Segoe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em; font-weight: 700; =
line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>What is bill.com?&nb=
sp;</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: =
26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.com is founder René Lacerte’s sec=
ond bite at the apple. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In 1999, Lacerte, a fourth generat=
ion entrepreneur, left his job at Intuit to build PayCycle and replace manu=
al payroll with software. It looked like this: </p><div class=3D"capti=

O role, Lacerte served as CFO for a year before leaving the company to work=
 on his next thing.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-he=
ight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Lacerte’s next thing, which he founde=
d in 2006, was originally called “CashView.” When Salesforce CE=
O Marc Benioff heard about it, he called Lacerte and offered him, for the l=
ow low price of $200k, a domain that he happened to own: bill.com. </p=
><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 =
0 1em 0;">Bill.com’s mission was and is to “make it simple to c=
onnect and do business.” It attempts to do that through cloud-based A=
ccounts Receivable (AR) and Accounts Payable (AP) software for SMBs. <=
/p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: =
0 0 1em 0;">If it sounds unsexy, that’s because it is, but it’s=
 also important. In any business, particularly SMBs, cash is king. A compan=
y can improve its cash position without doing a dollar more in sales or cut=
ting any costs by stretching out payables and pulling in receivables. There=
’s even a formula for it: the Cash Conversion Cycle formula. </p=
><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" style=3D"font-size: 16px; =

x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The prize for helping companies s=
implify AR and AP and improve the cash conversion cycle is large: there are=
 6 million small or medium businesses (SMBs), those with revenues under $10=
0 million, in the United States, and 75-90% of those businesses still pay f=
or things with checks. Over the next decade, most of that volume will move =
online. </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" style=3D"f=

 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Lacerte ran essentially the s=
ame playbook at PayCycle and bill.com:</p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li =
style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p sty=
le=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Repla=
ce a paper-based finance workflow with software.</p></li><li style=3D"margi=
n: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #=
1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Distribute through =
partnerships with accounting firms and others who own the relationships wit=
h SMB clients. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; ms=
o-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; lin=
e-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Try to create network effects and switching cos=
ts and hold on for dear life.</p></li></ul><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Because PayCycle and bi=
ll.com competed with physically writing out paychecks and invoices, they ha=
d a low product quality bar to jump over. So they built something functiona=
l and tried to get it in the hands of as many people as possible as quickly=
 as possible in a race to create network effects. </p><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The foc=
us on distribution over product shows. Even bullish articles about the comp=
any, like a16z’s <em><a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxF=
UMuOhCAQ_JrhtoaXigcOe9nfIC30KBkFgzgT9-u3ZzxsIF1NP6hUeag45XLaLe-VvYOr54b0DB6=
ocuxYXAxWdNKoVg8sWB2EaQ2Lu7sXxBXiYms5kG3HuEQPNeb02eBctmy2HMxowCiAsR1QglRdp3=
VPB_vB9_eLFI4QMXm0-MRy5oRssXOt235T3zf5QxdE99v4vFIqueQEXL5zTSFhfeXy-AolPjF9T=
SW_6kx1Fi3NCi64FlqJtm1EY0y_hGnjKvib5uskm_0Y9wr-8f6dFZtyHXOJaaL2Y4YVl0-D5DnC=
9Uixng4TjAuGS3m93Pt44SZMWMjV4KCSbXzoe94TGnEpJWsUH5SRQjBiDpm20j_pH-zXhJ8" st=
yle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">When Distribution Trump=
s Product</a>, </em>concede that “while <strong>the company’s U=
I leaves much to be desired</strong>, its core product—sending paymen=
ts and getting paid—works efficiently.”</p><p style=3D"color: #=
1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I have some=
 thoughts on the UI, but we’ll get to that in a bit. For now, let&#82=
17;s stick to the facts: the product, distribution, and business model.</p>=

 1em 0;"><strong>Product </strong></p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">So what does bill.com d=
o? Simply, it lets small businesses pay vendors, and lets vendors invoice s=

</div><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margi=
n: 0 0 1em 0;">The graphic shows the <strong>Accounts Payable</strong> pers=
pective. Instead of the finance team receiving a paper invoice, emailing a =
business lead for approval, writing and mailing a physical check, and manua=
lly recording the transaction in the company’s accounting software, t=
hey just get a digital invoice, click approve, click pay, and they’re=
 done. Simple! </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-h=
eight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">My experience with the product is on the <=
strong>Accounts Receivable</strong> side. When a company sponsors Not Borin=
g, I create an invoice on bill.com, write a message, hit send, and then sit=
 back while I wait for the company to pay. Simple! </p><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Theore=
tically, it’s <em>all</em> simple and easy. By connecting small busin=
esses with their customers and vendors, bill.com wants to make it easy to p=
ay and get paid, lower cash conversion cycles for its customers, and help S=
MBs thrive. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-heig=
ht: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>Distribution</strong></p><p style=3D"=
color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bil=
l.com puts most of its eggs in the distribution basket. It’s the weap=
on with which it’s chosen to go to war against the status quo and new=
 competitors. That distribution comes through four main channels: </p>=

8m_4BQ_x-HQ" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">Seema Am=
ble</a> writes about the five ways that bill.com fuels network-driven growt=
h, which map to the four channels:</p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong=
>Minimize Friction</strong> (Direct to SMB). Bill.com’s online platfo=
rm removed a ton of friction from the traditional paper and mail process.</=
p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bull=
et;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin=
: 0;"><strong>Require Low Commitment, Deliver High Value</strong> (Direct t=
o SMB). It’s free to start using bill.com, so it can prove value befo=
re charging subscription and transaction fees.</p></li><li style=3D"margin:=
 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Compel Growth=
 Through Design Choices</strong> (Direct to SMB). If a customer sends a ven=
dor an invoice, the vendor needs to set up an account to pay online. Simila=
rly, a vendor can invite a payee to create an account to receive payment. T=
his should create virality. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.7=
5px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Identify Adjacent Growth C=
hannels</strong> (Accounting Firms & Accounting Software Providers) Bil=
l.com sells into accounting firms (of which 4,000 use bill.com), who then g=
et their clients to use bill.com. “More than 50 percent of Bill.com&#=
8217;s customers and 45 percent of revenue are derived from its accountant =
partners.”</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-sp=
ecial-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-he=
ight: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Tackle Larger Distribution Nodes</strong> (=
Financial Institution Partners). Bill.com works with six of the ten largest=
 US banks, who use bill.com to power better AR/AP solutions for their busin=
ess banking clients. Partnerships like these are only possible with the sca=
le of customers that bill.com built up via Direct to SMB and Accounting par=
tnerships. </p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; =
line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Today, 103k customers use bill.com t=
o accept payments from a network of 2.5 million suppliers. This is Bill&#82=
17;s <strong>network effect</strong>: as more customers and suppliers use b=
ill.com, the experience becomes better for every new company and supplier t=
hat joins, because they should be able to easily find and pay each other se=
amlessly. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height=
: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Then there’s the <strong>switching costs<=
/strong>: I’ve actually tried to stop using bill.com twice, and both =
times, I was brought back into the fold when a sponsor told me, “Sorr=
y, we have to use bill.com here and it would be easier to pay you if you in=
voiced us there.” </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-st=
atic" style=3D"font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin-bottom: 16px; tex=

1em 0;">Nothing like the threat of not getting paid to keep you from switch=
ing. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26p=
x; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>Business Model</strong></p><p style=3D"color=
: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.com=
’s business model is straightforward. It makes money in three ways:&n=
bsp;</p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7=
=2E5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p=20=
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 1=
6px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Subscription </strong>(53% of r=
evenue)<strong>.</strong> Monthly plans start as low as $39/mo/user. &=
nbsp;</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-forma=
t: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0;"><strong>Transactions </strong>(33% of revenue). Bill collects =
fees for every transaction completed through the platform. </p></li><l=
i style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><st=
rong>Float </strong>(13% of revenue). Bill earns interest while payments ar=
e clearing. In my experience, that takes about a week each time. </p><=
/li></ol><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; ma=
rgin: 0 0 1em 0;">The business model is strong on paper: it combines the pr=
edictability of monthly recurring revenue with the upside of transaction fe=
es. It grows as it adds new customers, as its customers add more users, and=
 as they make or accept more payments through the platform. </p><p sty=
le=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0=
;">In the 12 months ended September 2020, it brought in $169 million in rev=
enue, 86% of which came from subscription and transaction fees (float tanke=
d as interest rates did). 86% of that core revenue came from existing custo=
mers, thanks to a strong 121% dollar-based net retention rate, a good sign =
that the company is able to lock in customers and grow with them. The netwo=
rk effects and switching costs seem to be working. </p><div class=3D"c=

e-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The numbers show a core business that&#=
8217;s solid if not spectacular. Analysts cite potential upside from new pr=
oducts like virtual card issuance, cross-border transactions, and instant t=
ransfer. The 121% dollar-based net retention rate is a good signal that the=
 distribution → network effect lock-in strategy is working.</p><p sty=
le=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0=
;">The market seems to like what it sees. Bill.com went public on December =
12, 2019 at a $1.6 billion market cap on Q1 2020 revenues of $35.2 million =
and net losses of $5.7 million. A little over a year later, on Q1 2021 reve=
nues of $46.2 million (31% higher) and net losses of $12.9 million (56% hig=
her), <strong>BILL is trading at an $11.1 billion market cap (7x higher)</s=
trong>! </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: =
26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Something’s off. I don’t think anyone=
 on Wall Street has ever actually had to use bill.com… </p><h3 s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-system, B=
linkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, 'Apple=
 Color Emoji', 'Segoe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em; fon=
t-weight: 700; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>The B=
ill Bear Case</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; lin=
e-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">On paper, bill.com’s strategy is =
elegant. Through smart distribution, it’s overcome a mediocre product=
 and built a real network effect and switching costs. It worked in the 1990=
’s, why can’t it work now? </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a;=
 font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Well, in 2021, BIL=
L is a bubble begging to be popped. As if to invite the dot com bubble comp=
arison openly, it even has “.com” in its name. Who does that an=
ymore?! </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: =
26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">BILL is overvalued, and not just in the “oh=
 lol 2020 got a little out of hand” sense. Bill.com is overvalued eve=
n relative to all of the other SaaS or fintech stocks that exploded in 2020=
, and unlike most of them, the product sucks, making its future much dimmer=
=2E </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a;=
 font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; =
margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Ultimately, the bear case boils down to two things:&nbs=
p;</p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5=
px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>The Numbers.</strong> This one is=
 so straightforward that I thought I was missing something. Compared to all=
 of its comps, bill.com is too expensive. Normally, I’d squint my way=
 past that and imagine a world in which it grows into the valuation, if it =
weren’t for… </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px=
 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>The Product. </strong>Bill.co=
m’s user experience is terrible. I know, I know, AR and AP are really=
 hard. But at an $11 billion market cap in the year of our lord 2020, a goo=
d tech company’s job is to abstract away all of that complexity. Bill=
=2Ecom makes it clear that it’s ha=
rd. That makes me very skeptical that=
 bill.com will be able to put up numbers that justify that valuation any ti=
me soon. </p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; li=
ne-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I’ll say upfront here that most =
analysts who cover the stock are bullish. According to <a href=3D"https://e=
mail.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMtuwyAQ_JpwiwUYx-TAoT1UqpR_QDw2NgoGC3Al_303dqV=
KiGFnmR3tONNgymVXa66NvC_d9hWw9M4gs1UoOnjFblz2g7gTr4RncpAkVP0sAIsJUbWyAVk3G4=
MzLeR0KCjlA5mVoxSoZfJ5EyMTlo18GC0Y6qk0xovnaWo2HyA5UPADZc8JSFRza2u99B8X_oWnA=
rxCmkxcZ9O5vLypfbE54uPz-_FAKGiepnp8jvFag4frH0eC4pQzyqhgomfD0LFOyjH6aaW9dxdB=
l4l3dbO1Gfd6zydFpdxsLijH9ms2C8SjgYtqxGVLoe0akrER_JlBO3M8UtETJEB78No0DJDex5G=
OiJKdO2NIPb33kjNG0NlnVKV_018IMIsv" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration=
: underline;">SeekingAlpha</a>, of thirteen sell-side ratings, seven are ve=
ry bullish, two are bullish, and four are neutral. They get paid to do this=
 for a living, and you should track down their reports for a balanced persp=
ective. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: =
26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I don’t buy it. Bill.com has already outrun=
 their bullish average $125 targets and the multiples are too damn high!&nb=
sp;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; marg=
in: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>The Numbers </strong></p><p style=3D"color: #1=
a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.com is =
wildly overvalued because it benefited from some uniquely 2020 tailwinds: n=
amely, the <strong>surge in fintech</strong> broadly and <strong>investors&=
#8217; ravenous hunger for lower-market cap tech companies</strong>. </p><p=
 style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1=
em 0;">In FY 2017, bill.com generated $64 million in revenue, growing to $1=
08 million in FY 2018 and $157 million in FY 2019. That’s good (if sl=
owing) growth, but it’s nothing compared to the stock price’s r=
ise. Bill.com shot up 258% in 2020 (and that after a drop; it was up 297% w=
hen I started researching this piece). </p><div class=3D"captioned-ima=

align: center;"></td></tr></tbody></table></figure></div><p style=3D"color:=
 #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Even craz=
ier, that outperformance came despite the fact that while COVID accelerated=
 growth in many of the companies in the BVP Index, it hurt or fatally wound=
ed many of bill.com’s SMB customers. As a result, bill.com’s re=
venue growth <em>slowed </em>while other fintech companies’ soared. L=
et’s compare it to Square, another fintech focused on SMBs, for examp=
le:  </p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px=
 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; f=
ont-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Both stocks performed about =
the same in 2020, with BILL coming out slightly ahead of SQ at 258.7% vs. 2=
41.0%. Unlike BILL, SQ has the numbers to back it up.</p></li><li style=3D"=
margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Between Q3 201=
9 and Q3 2020, <strong>Square’s revenue grew an astounding 140%.</str=
ong> Square’s revenue is weird, because when people buy Bitcoin on Ca=
sh App, it books the full amount as revenue, so to be charitable to bill.co=
m, let’s look at gross profits. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.=
75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1=
a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Square grew gros=
s profits 59%</strong> YoY to $794 million in Q3 2020, accelerating from 41=
% growth in the same period the previous year. <strong>Bill.com’s gro=
ss profit, meanwhile, grew 31% YoY</strong>, a sharp deceleration from the =
62% growth it saw over the same period the year before. </p></li><li s=
tyle=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p styl=
e=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><stron=
g>Square was profitable</strong> last quarter, with $36.5 million in net in=
come. <strong>Bill.com was twice as </strong><em><strong>unprofitable </str=
ong></em>as it was the same quarter the previous year, with $12.9 million i=
n net losses. </p></li></ul><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.com is growing more slowly t=
han Square (and that growth is decelerating) off a much smaller base. Its p=
roduct is worse. It doesn’t have the talent density or design focus t=
hat Square does. It serves a much smaller customer base, putting it at a di=
stribution disadvantage. It doesn’t have a growth engine like Cash Ap=
p waiting in the wings. It doesn’t have a $50 million Bitcoin investm=
ent that’s doubled since October. It’s losing money. And yet, w=
hile <strong>BILL’s EV/Annualized Revenue is 54.6x, Square’s EV=
 / Annualized </strong><em><strong>Gross Profit</strong></em><strong> is 30=
=2E9x. That’s crazy. </strong=
></p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>Here’s wh=
at I think is happening</strong>, and believe me, I know this sounds stupid=
, but I think it’s also true: investors wanted to buy fintech stocks,=
 saw that BILL only had a market cap of like $4 billion versus SQ at $50 bi=
llion earlier in the year, and figured that BILL had a lot more room to run=
=2E $1 trillion divided by $4 billion is=
 250, and $1 trillion divided by $50 =
billion is only 20, ipso facto, more upside. Or something like that. <=
/p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" style=3D"font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin-bottom: 16px; text-align: center;"><figure styl=

t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">To believe that bill.c=
om has a shot at growing into its inflated valuation any time soon, you nee=
d to believe that it’s going to re-accelerate growth and that it&#821=
7;s going to keep growing for a long time. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1=
a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I’m, uhh=
h, skeptical, because bill.com’s product doesn’t feel like a pr=
oduct built in or for this decade, and it’s going to face competitors=
 whose products do. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; l=
ine-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>The Product</strong></p><p st=
yle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em =
0;">Bill.com is the worst software I use to run Not Boring. It takes someth=
ing that should be joyful -- getting paid! -- and makes it dreadful. That p=
resents an opening for product-first competitors to cut off bill.com’=
s growth and pick off customers. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-=
size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The idea for bill.com&#8=
217;s product sounds simple -- send and receive invoices and payments onlin=
e -- and the product looks clean enough on the surface, but in my experienc=
e, there are a host of little things that add up to a poor product experien=
ce. </p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.7=
5px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Poor Communication. </stro=
ng>When a sponsor pays me, there’s no communication or notification. =
The money just kind of floats in the ether. To find out where it is, I need=
 to contact them through the “Message Center.”</p></li><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong=
>Confusing UI.</strong> Often, a sponsor will pay me outside of bill.com, a=
nd the invoice sits there, marked as unpaid and overdue. It took me half an=
 hour of clicking around to figure out how to mark the invoice “Paid.=
”</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-for=
mat: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26p=
x; margin: 0;"><strong>Issues with Multiple Bank Accounts. </strong>One of =
my bank accounts was approved, another wasn’t, or something, and when=
 I tried to switch, without warning, I was no longer able to receive paymen=
ts. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-f=
ormat: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 2=
6px; margin: 0;"><strong>Poor Onboarding and Late Verification.</strong> In=
stead of verifying my identity upfront, I got a message in my Message Cente=
r asking me to upload my driver’s license and incorporation docs afte=
r I’d already completed a few transactions and was in the middle of a=
nother one, delaying payment. This could have been solved by a walkthrough =
at signup, a very normal feature in modern software. </p></li><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong=
>Hard to Connect Directly with Vendor.</strong> Unless you have the right e=
mail address upfront when you create a vendor account, good luck figuring o=
ut how to connect to the right vendor.</p></li></ul><p style=3D"color: #1a1=
a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">There are more=
, but you get the point. To be clear, none of these issues is insurmountabl=
e, and if I worked in AP I’m sure I’d spend the time to get rea=
lly good at bill.com, but in 2021, the counterintuitiveness of the product =
is striking. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Modern software, even B2B SaaS, is supposed =
to be intuitive. Magical little details that work <em>better </em>than expe=
cted are the norm. That’s the promise of the consumerization of enter=
prise SaaS: finally, work products that we love as much as the products we =
choose to use as consumers. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Today, a great product experi=
ence is table stakes. Or as <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJx=
FkEuOwyAQRE8TlhZgHOMFi9nMNRCfjoNigwXtWL79dOLFSIhuKJVKr4JDmEs9zVYass9l8dyAnj=
E4-tkbVJuiEXep-0FNLBoVhR40S80-KsDq0mKw7sC23S8pOEwlfx2cy4E9zUN47ZUWMEXJQT70X=
YIDyb3jMSoRrlC3xwQ5gIE31LNkYIt5Im7t1v_c5C-d4zi6lN_QMJSVPFA7WkhgyUguBRdcCdWL=
YehEp_W4xHnjfQw3xddZdm33DV14fUysmlzQl5ryTPLr6VZYvgIxWJrrnhOeFrLzC8QLD6-KvsB=
2hgyVqovWIXXDp3HkI00tLhzi7_nUaykEo-RYyJX_Q_8ANyZ_Dg" style=3D"color: #1a1a1=
a; text-decoration: underline;">Composer</a> founder <a href=3D"https://ema=
il.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkEuKxDAMRE_TXgZ_E2fhxWzmGsYfddq0YwdHmSG3H3eyGBASk=
iiKV8EhLLWdZqs7kk-zeG7Q1xhcvxw7NJuiYSPXQsmZRCMj00qTtNtnA1hdygbbAWQ7fE7BYarl=
UlDKFXmZ8anBgw_jJBRIPyugPI7POcjgJubobeqOmKAEMPAD7awFSDYvxG1_iK8H_-6FvwkR2hD=
q2jcPpdWcoSFJhlPOKKOSScGUGtig9ZTjslERw0PSdeHDfvgdXXh_5KSZUtHXlsrS3--XWyFfj4=
5g-1yPkvC0UJzPEG86vBO6eO0CBVpPLlqHPRo6TxOd-tTspun4gs5Cc8ZId461q8q_6R_Wgn7c"=
 style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">Ben Rollert</a> put =
it so succinctly: </p><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #67b=
dfc; margin: 1em 0;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-heig=
ht: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>Product is just somethi=
ng that can’t be off, whereas distribution can be figured out. <stron=
g>If there aren’t core product chops or an appreciation of it, a star=
tup is basically fucked.</strong> So even if distribution is as important i=
n the end, or even more important, it isn’t as much of a precondition=
=2E</em></p></blockquote><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-he=
ight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">While bill.com has chosen a distribution-fi=
rst strategy typical of early B2B SaaS products, its competitors are buildi=
ng product-first, backed by strong distribution. For bill.com to ever have =
a shot at growing into its valuation, it will somehow need to fend off thos=
e competitors.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height:=
 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">A couple of potential threats, namely Square and=
 Stripe, are particularly dangerous for Bill.com because they’re prod=
uct-first companies that have added powerful distribution to their arsenals=
=2E On a 2018 <a href=3D"https://email.m=
g2.substack.com/c/eJxFkMtuwyAQRb_G7Go=
NYGJ7waJRFPUlVVUfi24sbCYOjQ0W4LbO15fEi0qIGe7M1RWnUxF75xc5uRDJ5WriMmF66k4lZQ=
7oG6Ml3bCKi6ImWhaaVqIiJjQHjzgqM8joZyTT3A6mU9E4e3UAMEGOEnnablsNKtkoCA24qatat=
FTxEli9hqpZG7QdSvxGvziLZJDHGKeQ8duM7dNxE9o8TC6aw5J3bkwSTiY4jakrzvD5O2yEB1b0=
9n57erl7fNpmfB9MxnfnZ_M-qu3H28dN_bUDW78-9PSHGMmAUaBQ0IJTIXKaV1U56H4CrrusgLF=
neZjbEFV3ukQSL62LrfPG9ml8OqoRh-sg_b1JdZytiUuDVrUD6hVLXNFeQTU9WvQJuW5UTEyhLk=
soU63oiiFx41DzilFKUrJ2yWX_Q_8AS2WO7g" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decorat=
ion: underline;">podcast</a> I’ve quoted a couple of times, Patrick C=
ollison told Tim Ferriss: </p><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px so=
lid #67bdfc; margin: 1em 0;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; l=
ine-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>If they [startu=
ps] can create a product that is so much better than the status quo that th=
ey start to get organic traction, once you attach a real sales and marketin=
g engine to that, it’s going to be really frickin hard for a big comp=
any to effectively compete because this organizational transformation to be=
ing good at software is just profoundly hard.</em></p></blockquote><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"=
>Neither Stripe nor Square focuses heavily on invoicing yet, and neither ha=
ve built products with the full capabilities of bill.com’s, but both =
do offer invoicing products and bring an army of existing customers to bear=
, and can use their own distribution to come after bill.com. </p><ul s=
tyle=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-spe=
cial-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 26px; margin: 0;">Square had 350,000 active sellers using its Invoices=
 product in Q1 2019, more than 3x bill.com’s, and has shown an abilit=
y and desire to offer a variety of products and integrations to help small =
businesses grow. If invoicing is really a market big enough to support an $=
11 billion company, I have no doubt Square will make a bigger push into Bil=
l’s territory. </p></li></ul><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li st=
yle=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Stripe =
wants to build the economic infrastructure for the internet, and has millio=
ns of customers, all of whom could choose to use <a href=3D"https://email.m=
g2.substack.com/c/eJxFUEGOwyAMfE05RkCgoQcOe9lvIMBuipZABGSl_H7d5rCSZWtsjcYz0=
Q9cazvtXvtg7-bGuSNBiJ42R8fmElhxl2bW6sHAKhBGG5a6ezbEzadsRzuQ7UfIKfqRavkwOJea=
vWz0T6UhLPIeniAW8Bo1aC65ECrifL9E_QEJS0SLv9jOWpBl-xpj77f56ya_qfpoaccp1o1ASDm=
nsrJkJZeCC66EmoXWk5iMWTKsO58h3hTfVjn1I_Th48-by5otdYTaiE3nn5ffMH8O9L6juR0ljd=
Nh8SEjXM7Glc7Hq1uxYKPUwPlBsfDHsvCFphGXE7I-88dspBCMlKESq_yL_gFDu3zD" style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">Stripe Billing</a> for inv=
oicing instead of bill.com to keep their finances in one place. Even a phot=
ographer we just worked with sent us a Stripe invoice. It was lovely and si=
mple. Plus, via API, Stripe could give all of Shopify’s merchants whi=
te-labeled invoicing, all within their Shopify account.</p></li></ul><p sty=
le=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0=
;">And it’s not just the fintech sueprstars. On a recent episode of <=

<p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0=
 1em 0;">The best performer and second-most-hated, Atlassian (TEAM) is up a=
 whopping 753%. On average, the group is up 231.6%, compared with 82.6% for=
 the S&P 500 and 157.4% for the Nasdaq. The only negative performer on =
the list, IBM, is only there because it bought Lotus Notes, and people stil=
l hate Lotus Notes. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; l=
ine-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>If you bought an equally-weig=
hted basket of the companies that make the software that business users hat=
e the most five years ago (and added when newly public companies IPO’=
d), you would have outperformed the S&P by 4x and the Nasdaq by half!</=
strong> That’s a good hedge fund. How is that possible? </p><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em=
 0;">There are a few reasons. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-siz=
e: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">First things first, to beat=
 the naysayers to it, this data is incredibly messy and imprecise. My twitt=
er universe is not a representative sample. Some of these companies are mas=
sive and also make products that people love. Microsoft users hate Windows,=
 Teams, and Dynamics, but they <em>love</em> Excel. Slack is near the top o=
f both the most-hated and <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFk=
E1uxCAMhU8TlhG_ARYsKlWz6xkiAk4GTQIRIa1y-7qTRSWEbezHk7_gGyylXm4vRyN_19iuHbCM=
wePLeUAdU3Rs4EYoaUl0MjKjDEnHOFeAzafVtXoC2c9pTcG3VPJbQSlX5InjUYVhnpie1Ky0tXI=
efPBz5DYMnKnb1J8xQQ7g4BvqVTKQ1T1b249OfHT8gaf9pNag9qFsWO0-vK4vTI7m23lgwoSUcr=
BSC8GsHgwdZCceqP_klCTHKWeUUcmkYEr1rDdGr3HZqYihk3RbeH-cE_4WXn8WpLpc2lRqygu2X=
0-_wfpu4Jojxu3MqV0jZD-tEG8C7ab4ZjIukKEi3Tj6hvio1ZpqjIbdGyMiQa0wnDGCzrGgKv-b=
_gJELoa3" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">most-loved<=
/a> lists. People love Google search and G Suite, even if some don’t =
like Hangouts. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-h=
eight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Secondly, even being included on the list =
multiple times means that a lot of people use the product. Over a billion p=
eople use Microsoft Office; some are bound to hate it. On the flip side, I =
suspect that bill.com got just two votes because it only has 103k customers=
=2E It received no “most-loved&#82=
21; votes. </p><p style=3D"color=
: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">A bigger=
 reason, though, is that most of the companies on this list are old. The av=
erage age of companies on the list is 34. They built moats during an era in=
 which purchasing decisions were made centrally and the end-users, the empl=
oyees, were just forced to use software that wasn’t very good. Back t=
hen, the B2B playbook worked:</p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"=
margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Replace a manu=
al process with software that was good enough but not great</p></li><li sty=
le=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Use lar=
ge and expensive sales forces and partnerships to sell into IT departments&=
nbsp;</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-forma=
t: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0;">Build moats -- mainly <strong>network effects</strong> and <st=
rong>switching costs</strong> -- to lock companies in.</p></li></ul><p styl=
e=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;=
">The historical winners in B2B software famously and unabashedly focused o=
n distribution over product. </p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong=
>Oracle</strong> famously sold vaporware to companies and governments knowi=
ng that the contracts and switching costs would lock customers in long enou=

tyle=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p styl=

FUMuOwyAM_JpyjHiEQg4c9tLfQA44CWoCESEr5e_XbQ4rWbY81mg8E6DhXOrl9nI09mm-XTvSGg=

KLHpoFAofjOGGphW3DzKu-KCsFIKRcizEyv-if3Tbey4" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text=
-decoration: underline;">Roam</a></strong> raised a <em>seed round</em> at =
a $200 million valuation in September.  </p></li><li style=3D"mar=
gin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color:=
 #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">On the public sid=
e, <strong>Slack</strong> was the fastest SaaS company ever to hit $100 mil=
lion in revenue, and recently sold to Salesforce (SAD!) for $27.7 billion.&=
nbsp;</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-forma=
t: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0;">We all know what happened to <strong>Zoom</strong>’s sto=
ck in 2020 (just in case: it rose 395%). </p></li></ul><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Produc=
t-first with built-in distribution is the new model. Customers love it. Inv=
estors love it. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-=
height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">A distribution-first company like bill.co=
m would never even get off the ground in 2021. We’re past that. That =
brings us to the concluding question: is it locked in enough at this point =
that it’s going to stick around forever like Microsoft, Salesforce, a=
nd Oracle, or do competitors still have a chance to take it down? </p>=
<h3 style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-syst=
em, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, '=
Apple Color Emoji', 'Segoe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em=
; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>=
Killing a Baby Dictator</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">There’s a question that=
 ethicists like to pose over a couple of drinks: if you could go back in ti=
me and kill a dictator / terrorist / murderer when they were still a baby, =
would you do it? </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line=
-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">We can’t go back and kill SAP, Sal=
esforce, or Microsoft. The switching costs are too high, the moats are too =
deep, and companies are too locked in. Those companies’ customers wil=
l have to die out for their products to die out. But it’s not too lat=
e to stop bill.com. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; l=
ine-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I think what ultimately offends me so=
 much about Bill’s product experience is that it’s the first ti=
me I’ve encountered a <em>new</em> work product that wasn’t wel=
l-designed in a long time. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: =
16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I <em>expect</em> Salesforce t=
o suck. It’s been around for a while, since before the software we us=
e at work was supposed to be good, and it’s too late to do anything b=
ut build better interfaces on top. Salesforce got big enough, fast enough t=
hat we’re stuck with it. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But bill.com has a 2006-vi=
ntage product with slow enough growth that most potential customers are jus=
t finding out about it in 2020. It only has 103k customers in a market that=
 it says is 6 million potential customers strong. For anyone, like me and t=
he 5.9 million other target customers just discovering bill.com from today =
onward, the product experience is jarring. It’s like walking into a M=
ercedes dealership today and having the salesperson pass off an obviously u=
sed 2006 E-Class as the newest model. </p><div class=3D"captioned-imag=
e-container-static" style=3D"font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin-bot=

gure></div><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; =
margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.com has left a wide opening for new entrants to co=
me in and build better AR/AP software that makes getting paid fun and helps=
 companies better manage their cash conversion cycles. </p><p style=3D=
"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">To=
 knock off bill.com, Square, Stripe, Settle, or a new, product-first compet=
itor will need to: </p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margi=
n: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #=
1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Build a seamless pr=
oduct to feature parity with bill.com on the things that matter to customer=
s. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-fo=
rmat: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26=
px; margin: 0;">Build distribution into the product workflows. </p></l=
i><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;">=
<p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"=
>Build up enough customer demand to attract enough vendors and partners ont=
o the platform that Bill’s switching costs and network effects are re=
ndered obsolete.  </p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-=
size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">With those companies&#82=
17; product cadence and 5.9 million customers ripe for the picking, many of=
 whom have moved online during the pandemic, I like their odds. When they d=
o, they’ll limit Bill’s upside and make it impossible for the c=
ompany to grow into its impossibly high valuation. </p><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Even a=
fter a wild run in 2020, I’m still incredibly bullish on tech compani=
es. Product-first companies with built-in distribution will create the next=
 generation of $100 billion+ market cap giants. Bet on those companies, eve=
n if their market caps are much higher than bill.com’s.</p><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"=
>As for bill.com? I’m not shorting, because shorting anything in a ma=
rket like this is asking to burn money, and there’s always the risk t=
hat one of the old guard sees a younger version of itself in bill.com&#8217=
;s product and buys it. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Instead, I’m considering se=
lling long-dated OTM calls. Heads, bill.com tanks or stays flat, I win. Tai=
ls, bill.com rockets another 50%, and I lose some money, but I can only ima=
gine how well the other companies in my portfolio would be doing. It’=
s a small hedge on my very overweight tech portfolio. </p><p style=3D"=
color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Plu=
s, if the trade works out, it will be the easiest time I’ve ever had =
getting paid via bill.com… </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-con=
